comments.title: Red bags; GFS Initiation
comments.content:
Good morning JMG, and everybody else!

No replies up yet, and you‘re in a creative mood - I hope that bears well both for the amount of sleep you got, and for your next book(s)… ;-)

Thanks, as always, for having us over today and patiently answering all the questions. I really learn a lot each week, and treasure this space very much.

A few questions today, if I may (however many you feel ready to answer):

About the red bags and jars with salt/nail:

1. Is it advisable to wear them/put them up all the time, or would this be too much? Or, in other words, does the „occult immune system“ need some training, and constant „disinfection“ isn‘t healthy either?

2. I presume there is some (healthy) energy exchange e.g. between close couples or between parents and their children. Does a red bag suppress or prevent this? Could wearing such a bag lead to issues in that regard?

3. Since a lot of people don‘t even seem to be aware that they are drawing energy from other people: How does one notice? Does this directly correlate with e.g. drawing attention or time, being needy, being dependent, etc? Or are there other, less obvious, cues to look out for in oneself?

And about the GSF Initiation and lodge:

4. In the initiation, there is a composition of place, but no un-composition. One second, you‘re in the grove, and the next second, you‘re back in the lodge, just like that. Does that have a specific reason, or did it just happen to end up that way?

5. Why is the lodge closing done in the same order as the opening, and not in reverse? I.e. why doesn‘t one „unroll“ things in reverse order?

Thanks, and if you and your wife would have them, my blessings to both of you - may you have a healthy, enjoyable and productive week!

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Red bags; GFS Initiation
replies.content:

3) It takes practice, but one useful hint is to watch what happens when you're alone for a while. If you start feeling depleted, and perk up suddenly when you get near another person, you may be parasitizing them.

4) So that the composition flows into the astral structure of the lodge itself.

5) What a fine theme for meditation! ;-)

replies.content: 3. This is why extroverts must be stopped. :)

replies.title: Re: Red bags; GFS Initiation
replies.content:

2) The jars in the house/flat corners would be less problematic then, I presume, at least as long as no people significantly close live elsewhere?

5) Harumph. Could‘ve seen this coming, of course. (I‘ll be back if it still doesn‘t make sense, though!! ;-) ).

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Red bags; GFS Initiation

Regular practice of a basic protective ritual such as the Sphere of Protection will teach you that, and so will most other courses of magical training, but it takes time and attention. comments.title: Personal Spiritual Path and Religious Organizations
comments.content:
Good Evening,

For my fellow Americans, a very happy Fourth of July. For everyone else in the northern hemisphere, a happy early-mid summer. For anyone in the southern hemisphere, I hope whatever stuff you're up to is good :)

To Share: A more personal post from me this time, I talk through my own spiritual path: https://jpowellrussell.com/#how_i_came_to_believe_what_i_do

To Ask: I'm thinking a lot about the role of religion in today's America, and into the future. I'm especially interested in the role of religious organizations. I feel like we all understand the role churches play/have played in the West and in America, but I'd be especially interested in hearing any thoughts on what other religious organizations may have been like/may be like today - for JMG or the commentariat.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Personal Spiritual Path and Religious Organizations
replies.content: The main alternative to churches has long been initiatory organizations. I knew a lot of old men in the  Masons and Odd Fellows for whom lodge meetings are their church services; I also know a lot of people in the occult scene for whom lodge meetings are central to their religious life. There are others, though -- it's an interesting question.

replies.title: Re: Personal Spiritual Path and Religious Organizations
replies.content:
Oh my, yes! The president of my grange said explicitly to several of us a few weeks ago, “The Grange is my church.”

I knew exactly what she meant because I’m finding it fills that role for me too (though I’m less involved than she is, she’s also active on the state level)

replies.title: Re: Personal Spiritual Path and Religious Organizations
replies.content:
If you watch TV or movies, pay attention to how many of them feature characters at AA-type meetings. There is a reason for that-- the 12 Step tradition is HUGE in California. When I lived in Santa Barbara there was something like 200 meetings every week. Tons of people were a part of it, including lots of people involved in the entertainment industry. Most of the people involved treated it either as church, or as the thing that was more important to them than Church even if they were a member of one.

In the groups had a very tribal spirit, which to my mind is very suggestive of "tamanous" does JMG has described it. Egalitarian, but with charismatic leaders dominating. A fission-fusion structure, like hunter-gatherer bands. An emphasis on personal spiritual experience. And strongly supportive networks formed among the members, who routinely found each other places to live, jobs, and so on.

To my mind it's a very American form of spirituality, democratic, egalitarian, franchisable, puritanical, bibliolatrous (every program has its Big Book). It has its drawbacks, but I can see a modified form of the 12 Step program having a big influence on the religions of the future on this continent, and I hope that it will.

replies.content: Huh, I didn't think about 12 Step Programs, but now that you mention them, I'm embarrassed I didn't, for all of the reasons you enumerate. Thank you for the avenue for further research!

Kaznacheev VP, Trofimov AV. Cosmic Consciousness of Humanity; Problems of New Cosmology. Elendis Progress Publishers, Tomsk, Russia, 1992.

Good day to you JMG!

Hello JMG!

I don't know if this belongs to your expertise and knowledge but I am looking for sources that will help the western reader understand and enter the occult world of Hinduism. It would be wonderful if you could recommend a book to me.

And a question from a friend of mine who is not here. What is your opinion on chaos magic (from the Carolla school). Can it be called , "effective" magic?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You'll have to ask someone who's studied Hindu occultism, which I haven't. Anyone have any suggestions?

2) Your friend reminds me of a once-famous poem:

"Last night I saw upon the stair,
A little man who wasn't there.
He wasn't there again today.
Oh, how I wish he'd go away!"

In answer to your friend's question, I'm not a fan. I read Carroll's Liber Null and Psychonaut when it was first published in 1987, and I also read its primary inspiration, the appendix on magic in Shea and Wilson's Illuminatus! trilogy, back in the 1970s. Every so often somebody comes up with something like this, a version of magic that's been stripped of everything offensive to modern materialist thinking; it's always very popular for a while, precisely because it doesn't challenge the conventional wisdom of its time, and so -- since such projects are always portrayed as radical, cutting-edge, rebellious, and exciting -- they allow people to pretend to be edgy while conforming to the official beliefs of their time. Some people apparently get good results from chaos magic; I find it resembles lite beer, compared to the good robust brown ale of more traditional approaches.

comments.title: Astral travel history and dangers

I read a fascinating, if chilling account of the 'astral war' between the Soviet Union and the USA during the cold war. The article is here if it is of interest to yourself and your readers https://medium.com/truly-adventurous/the-hades-environment-fab4434e8bdd

Essentially, it seems that both governments actively encouraged astral travel for various means, including warfare, but that chillingly, the amount of travelers awoke something ancient and nasty from the past, a name I won't repeat, which began killing people or trapping them whilst out of body.

It also seems that both governments became aware of the dangers, and rapidly closed down their programmes and actively censored the astral travel methodology that was in circulation at the time.

This all occurred around the time of the rise of the arch skeptics and angry atheists, and it has me wondering if that movement originally began as a psyop literally because of the dangers of the astral at that time. replies.title: Re: Astral travel history and dangers
replies.content:
I lived through those years of very wide-spread popular experimentation with astral travel and other such things. Certainly, now and then someone died experimenting with it, as with almost any esoteric technique. But most empahtically there was no effective censorship of how-to-do it books and articles: they could be widely found in pulp magazines and among cheap news-stand paperbacks, and academic research also continued in this or that university laboratory for a while. Some government agencies did indeed research such things for a while, with covert activity in view; and some of the resulting papers and documentation was eventually destroyed -- as much out of embarrassment at being thought to have been gullible as out of fear of their power in other hands.

The article strikes me as writtn not so much to inform as to titillate and chill.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Er, I recommend doing a little more historical research. The Golden Dawn didn't invent the circle with its four quarters; you can find that in Eliphas Lévi, who revived Western magic in the 1850s, and he drew on much older magical traditions. Here's a diagram of the magical cosmos from the writings of John Dee, Queen Elizabeth I's court wizard:

[IMAGE:0]

The division of the circle into four quarters -- not "corners" -- is found in Western occultism from early Greek times onward, and it's also common in many other parts of the world; for example, the Native American medicine wheel is similarly structured.

As for what would have happened if the Golden Dawn hadn't been founded, why, one of the dozens of other magical orders active at the same time, using very similar kinds of magic, would have filled the same role. The GD simply happened to be the one that most people borrowed from, and that's because its magic is very effective and tolerably easy to learn and use. I can't speak for Wicca, as I'm not into that; I gather that it's a popular source of inspiration because it was so popular in late 20th century America and so it's a convenient place to start. Again, though, there were other movements in the same direction that would likely have filled the same role.

That I thought was exclusively Golden Dawn.

I assumed the whole Hermetic Qaballah/Tarot/tracing pentagrams/Neo-Egyptian system was exclusively Golden Dawn, the way they mashed it all together in a workable system. Is that wrong? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Your best bet is to read up on other versions of magic available in the late 19th century. You might consider starting with Eliphas Lévi's Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic, which set the whole process in motion, then go on to The Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor by Joscelyn Godwin et al., and Morals and Dogma of the Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite by Albert Pike; those three are typical works of the period and will give you some idea of what magic was like in those days.

I have to wonder why this is considered important. Is it not analogous to bragging about having at least one branch in one's family tree trace back to ancient royalty? Quakers traditionally don't care about apostolic succession or even ordination, so this egalitarian background no doubt contributes to my confusion.

And I hope this question does not come across as disrespectful of the tradition. It's just that as the length of the chain (and the number of possible paths) tracing back to the original apostles continue to grow, it seems that by simple geometric progression, the number of possible paths to "valid apostolic succession" would increase tremendously and thus become less significant. Why does the idea still hold such importance, especially in a church that stresses independent, personal gnosis, not obedience to a set of rules allegedly tracing back to the year 32 AD? If I understand correctly, gnosis is not something handed down like a spiritual baton, which is how I'm stuck in thinking about apostolic succession. Thank you JMG.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's not about rules. Apostolic succession is a transmission of energy -- an empowerment. If passed on in the traditional manner, it makes it considerably easier to experience and transmit certain spiritual states which are conducive to gnosis. Is it possible to reach those states without apostolic succession? Sure, but if you have it, they're easier to attain and much easier to communicate to others.

Quakers, like so many of the movements that came out of the radical end of the Reformation, rejected apostolic succession and traditional ceremonial because it was so deeply tangled up in the power trips of the early modern Papacy and church hierarchy. That's understandable, but from a perspective within the independent sacramental movement (ISM), they threw out a treasure because they didn't like what had been done with the box that contains it. The whole point of the ISM is that it's possible to restore apostolic succession and the sacraments to what, from a Gnostic perspective, they once were: channels of power and blessing instituted by the Divine to assist us in the work of attaining gnosis.

Was there a time when worship was more of a thing or has religion always been just a way to control the population? Thanks, just looking for your thoughts because I respect your views on religion and faith.

replies.title: Re: Religion or set of ethical beliefs?
replies.content: The confusion between religion and ethics is mostly a habit of prophetic religions, i.e., those religions that were founded by a prophet, visionary, or messiah. Religions that grow up naturally among a people, like most of the old pagan faiths, don't have that habit. You can still find branches of Christianity that focus on worship and meditation rather than moral lectures, but you sometimes have to look hard, and Protestant churches are rarely a good place to start.

replies.title: Re: Changing luck
replies.content:
My very limited and distant understanding is that "luck" in a Southeast Asian, or at least East Asian, context has a little bit of a different spin than the Western half-conscious conception of an amorphous, impersonal force of favorable fortune. It'd be a little more like having various functionaries in a celestial bureaucracy pulling for you. Or maybe having the spirits who are administering your life and circumstances be more likely to be favorably disposed toward you. Or maybe having everything in the right order, such that things like prosperity and happiness would naturally tend to result as part of that order.

Perhaps these concepts could be adapted. The general sense seems to be that luck is a feature of a relationship. Although things capable of providing luck by a relationship are presumably also capable of having opinions or preferences about why not to confer what a human might consider "luck" under some select circumstances.

The idea of "right order" is related to Taoist practice especially, but is also similar to religious practice in general: one hears of miracles happing around a dedicated saint or mystic without them necessarily even having been themselves aware that there was a problem for a miracle to solve. Also the Egyptian deity Ma'at is a direct personification of one version of "right order", and Neoplatonism involves the sometimes-related idea of Logos.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation Questions
replies.content:
1) It's a little complex. At the end of each incarnation, the personality is absorbed into the Individuality and the bodies that once contained the personality dissolve. comments.title: Remediating a room with bad energy
comments.content:
A question to all and sundry:

Context: We've been in our current house for less than a year. It's a rental, and we hope to purchase a place of our own in the next couple years, so... not a permanent problem.

Problem: My bedroom, on the northwest corner of the house, has the crappiest energy. Rest of the house is fine. But I dislike being in my room so much that it's like... as soon as I walk through the door, I am sapped of all will to do anything constructive. Result: my room is a clutter disaster. The rest of the house, I can handle picking up, cleaning, as necessary. My room? Ugh. I just don't wanna be in there, and when I'm in there I don't wanna do anything. Instant defeat on crossing the threshold. Hard to sleep in there, too-- sometimes when it's really bad I just take my pillow and go sleep on the floor or a camp cot in the living room. I realize that clutter itself is reinforcing the problem, but I'm pretty sure the bad energy predated the clutter problem, and is the source of it.

Would appreciate any and all suggestions, advice, resources, for diagnosing the problem, and fixing it. Am (quite literally) tired of/from having a bedroom that's unpleasant to be/sleep in. I'm an introvert, I have kids, and I desperately need to have a pleasant non-public space in my house to retreat to.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On a mundane level: there's a great book called The House that Cleans Itself. Though the title is a bit of an overstatement, it's a tidiness book written for folks who are not naturally tidiers. The key insight from the book, which I've applied in lots of areas of my life is "make doing the right thing easier." So, if stuff is piling up, look at where it is piling up, and what kind of stuff clusters, and maybe you need something in that place helps you organize it a little better (stack of books? maybe a shelf above that table. Pile of mail? Put an inbox and a recycling bin there).

Spiritually/Energetically speaking: if you have a religious practice, consider praying for some help, maybe making a drink/incense offering in the space. We had a room in our house that had some bad energy that I became aware of shortly after starting my magical practice. I tried a banishing ritual and wasn't competent enough yet to get much of a result, so instead, I did some prayer, divination, and meditation about what was going on with the room, and then prayed to a few appropriate deities for some help, and the room became much better.

Whatever you try, good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You'll need to consult with someone who works with that end of astrology, and ask them. My astrological practice is based on late 19th and early 20th century techniques -- basically, what was in common use in the occult scene before astrologers went cuckoo over psychology -- and the almuten isn't a part of that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi anon, if I may—I've spent a lot of time on the "what would one do with such info" side of this!

According to some astrologers, the Almuten Figuris tells you the planet ruling your dæmon ("guardian angel"). This is a bit of lore derived from late Hellenistic philosophy; for example, Proclus tells us (in his commentary on Plato's First Alcibiades) that "about each of the divinities, there is an innumerable multitude of dæmons, and which are dignified with the same appellations as their leading gods: hence they rejoice when they are called by the names of Jupiter, Apollo, and Hermes, etc. as expressing the idiom, or peculiarity of their proper deities; and from these, mortal natures also participate of divine influxions." That is, dæmons tend to cluster about gods, and you are similarly suspended from a god by a particular one of these dæmons.

JMG also gives a method to determine this same information using geomancy in The Art and Practice of Geomancy. I've used both techniques (and a few others, in fact) and they unsurprisingly agree.

Your dæmon is your connection to the divine; so knowing the deity presiding over them tells you the nature of your connection to the divine. For example, my Almuten Figuris is Venus, and I'm a devotional mystic—no surprise there! The placement and condition of the planet in your natal chart also suggests how that works out for you in your present life; for example, an afflicted planet can indicate difficulties or setbacks in spiritual pursuits. You can also use this placement information to help you understand what kinds of spiritual pursuits you might want to undertake in life, or even use it to help you connect to your dæmon via prayer or theurgic methods. (Connecting with your dæmon is something I very much recommend, but of course, everyone's got their own practices, your mileage may vary, etc.)

I have a theory that the nature of one's mystical experiences tends to align with their Almuten Figuris. I've had a couple and they were both experiences related to seeing the beauty in everything, and the web of relationships by which those things were connected—obviously Venereal themes. (I have a couple data points from others that tentatively point in the same direction, but really not enough to make any firm conclusions about.)

I hope that's helpful to you!

comments.title: About Toxic Energies
comments.content:
Thank you for hosting these Magic Mondays and taking your questions. My question isn't about protection, as I am aware of the SoP ritual, but I would like to ask about the effects and nature of toxic energies.

Recently, I had to attend a weeks-long job training, and I work in a female-dominated field. Unfortunately, most of the women had extremely toxic personalities and partook in some of the nasty politics you commonly see on the Left nowadays, and they were most of the attendees. I started to sleep terribly. Throughout most of the day, I would be utterly exhausted, but then towards the end of the workday and when I arrived home, I would feel oddly awake and charged, but not in a terribly pleasant way. I am fairly new to spiritual practices but started to wonder if I was picking up toxic etheric and/or mental energies from them (because I, as a biological male am a receiver of those and they as biological females, emit them). While I am not ready to do the SoP, I do know how to do Jason Miller's Pillar and Spheres cleansing exercise, so I tried it. I immediately felt a lot cleaner. That night I slept like a baby and did so for most of the rest of the training.

Can toxic energies from people behave in this manner? I am curious if my hypothesis of events makes sense to someone more experienced.

replies.title: Re: About Toxic Energies
replies.content:
Yes, very much so. People who are sexually frustrated are generally miserable, and unless they have more than the usual amount of self-knowledge and self-mastery, they make everyone around them miserable, too. At the same time, women in that condition are going to have a lot of spare etheric energy. That's why Hitler, who was a capable occultist, used to have the SS fill the first few rows of the auditorium for his speeches with middle-aged women, whom they called "the varicose veins brigade"; their frustrated sexual energy and etheric force flowed toward him, and enabled him to work the magic that put millions of Germans in his power.

The cleansing exercise was a good thing to do. That enables you to benefit from the energy while clearing away the toxic emotions.

replies.title: Re: About Toxic Energies
replies.content:
It makes sense that Hitler might be counted as a capable occultist, but I'd be surprised, given what I remember reading from other sources, if he were also a conscious occultist. I thought people who had been close to him only described him mocking occultists.

It makes me think of Scott Alexander, who seems to have done a huge amount of something equivalent to discursive meditation, and is excellent at choosing words to "cause change in consciousness according to will", and generally has the effect of trying to make everything around him be normal and safe and sane, but is in almost all concrete ways a materialist. (He once told a story about being confused by a colleague's complaint about her patients having cathartic outbursts all the time and it being exhausting. His experience was that he couldn't follow his instructors' advice to try psychoanalytic methods that involved his patient having cathartic outbursts, because his patients didn't have cathartic outbursts, they just sort of rationally described what emotions they were feeling,)

replies.title: Re: About Toxic Energies
replies.content: No, he was mocking and dismissive about traditional occultism. He believed himself to be a modern, up-to-date, cutting-edge occultist, and had the same kind of attitudes about older forms of occultism that, say, a lot of chaos magicians have these days. He spent his time in Vienna reading all about occultism -- one of his friends from that period describes his reading, and he studied all the usual things -- and he was particularly interested in the teachings of Austrian occultist Joerg Lanz von Liebenfels, whose magazine Ostara Hitler read voraciously. Many of the other core Nazis from early on -- notably Himmler and Hess -- were up to their eyeballs in occult traditions, and of course the Nazi Party itself was launched as the political action wing of a magical lodge, the Thule-Gesellschaft.

replies.title: Re: About Toxic Energies
replies.content:
Huh. I'll have to look up those biographical points then, thank you.

It makes me wonder what kinds of things could go right or wrong in other movements if people make the right or wrong decisions about occult or religious practice in them, and what my responsibilities might be in connection with any movements I influence.

replies.title: Re: About Toxic Energies
replies.content: Those are good questions to ask yourself. I'd encourage any serious occultist to reflect on them, and to do some research into mages who failed disastrously, as Hitler did, to figure out what they did wrong.

I honor the Tuatha De Danaan in my Sphere of Protection and wondered if using Imblas when invoking Spirit Within rather than Awen would make sense. I haven’t experimented yet because my thought is that the world Awen has so much power because Druids around the world and across traditions all use it and it seems to resonate with other words like Amen and Aum. Imblas doesn’t seem to have those connections and doesn’t, to my ear at least, have the same resonance with other words. What are your thoughts?

2. I went to a four day yoga workshop with my wife a couple of weeks ago and in her lineage they associate the elements with different colors than I do and relate them to the chakras. I had the thought that, as an exercise, I would try to craft a Sphere of Protection that calls on the avatars of Shiva and Shakti (her lineage only recognizes these two divinities as the archetypal male and female). You’ve said many times that the SoP is very flexible. Is it flexible enough to accommodate the difference in symbolism such as Water being associated with the color Green and the Heart Chakra?

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: SoP Questions
replies.content:
1) Hmm. I'm pretty sure the word is Imbas, not Imblas, and either way I've never heard it treated as a sacred syllable. In your place I'd stay with Awen.

2) You could do that, but it would have much less power than something that keeps more of the traditional symbolism. You can certainly call on avatars of Shiva and Shakti in the six directions, but I recommend keeping the colors and symbols as they are.

comments.title: Severe Alcoholism and astral plane sight
comments.content:
Good evening and thanks for continuing to host this thread;

I have two separate queries today

  1. Regarding occulted aspects of severe alcoholism. I have a relative who has fallen into a state of severe addiction to hard liquor. In your view is someone in this state too dangerous to attempt a healing working on? I have a rudimentary understanding of druid magic.

  2. After years of discursive meditation, around forty grove openings, daily S.O.P and divination, and so on I have begun to see only barely into a different plane. I see something that looks like black moths that blip in and out of my perception. I also see translucent shapes that move it seems within another plane of existence. replies.title: Re: Severe Alcoholism and astral plane sight
replies.content:

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Severe dementia and extremely long lives run in my family. My natal chart has indicators of both as well, and having watched my grandfather slowly drift away, eventually no longer able to remember the woman he married over 60 years ago, no longer able to care for himself, nor to do anything other than exist, and continuing on like this for years, I know in my bones I don't want to suffer this fate. I'm considering my options, and while I'm working on keeping my mind and body healthy (using physical and metaphysical means), if this is genetic and I live long enough, then it may not work. Meanwhile, given the nature of the illness, I might not be coherent enough to realize there's a problem once it settles in.

I know this intention will likely shorten my life, possibly by decades; but I'd rather die right now than live with extreme dementia of the sort I watched some of my family go through towards the end. I don't want to suffer from it, nor do I want to subject anyone else to it. I pray to the planetary gods, and I'm thinking of asking Saturn, the ruler of old age, disability, and death, to make sure I die before my mind fails me. I'm also aware that as the ruler of karma, he might decide dementia is a necessary experience for me, and so might not help me, but if this is my karma then I think fighting it is a very bad idea.

Aside from the fact that I risk getting stuck in some sort of "astral waiting room" and that once I make this petition I'll need to be very respectful of Saturn (which seems like a good idea to me anyway), do you see any risks to this? I plan to get divination done, and to ask him what he thinks of this before making the request, but I'm not sure as yet as to my abilities on hearing what the gods are saying clearly, and I know divination isn't always totally reliable when strong emotions are involved, so I'd also like to know what you think of this as an expert on occultism and, given you once held a job in a nursing home, someone who knows what dementia looks like.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Get someone else to do the divination for you. If the reading says that this is appropriate, then, yes, you can go ahead with it. Be aware that Saturn may take you at your word and kill you sooner than you expect...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm willing to accept this risk. Death does not scare me as much as it does most people; I have no dependents; and I'll make sure to have my affairs in order before I make the petition in case Saturn does decide that it's the right time for me to die, and I'll make sure to keep them in order for the rest of my life.

All of this is assuming the divination comes back positive, of course.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You should probably be careful to think through multiple degrees of "my mind fails me", and consider how each of these degrees would relate to the exact wording of your petition and whether that's what you'd want in that case. If it weren't Saturn maybe you could expect more wiggle room in the interpretation.

In your position I wouldn't even go with that framing. I would look for a petition that delegates the decision to someone who can evaluate considerations of what consequences you wanted to prevent, from being alive with dementia, rather than just specifying that you not ever end up alive with dementia as such.

Honestly I don't understand how far in terms of steps of downstream consequences it's best to frame one's requests. Though, after a certain number of twists and turns, you start losing legibility of "oh, this effect was because of this request". That's a kind of a bad consequence in itself, since whoever's left doesn't get as good of feedback about what petitions to make.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Except, the entire reason prayer, rather than ritual, is what I'm considering here is that as a human being, I don't know the full outcome of anything: Saturn is in a better position to judge this, because he sees a lot more than I do, and he's able to understand what role everything plays in a way I will never be able to. So I want to leave it open-ended and allow Saturn to make decisions I wouldn't, because I will never be able to think of all the contingencies given my limited human mind.

Essentially, I'm placing my faith in him here: I trust that he will interpret this fairly, and frankly, if I didn't, I would not be putting my life into his hands. This is also why I said I plan to ask him about it first: he'll be able to tell me how he'll interpret the request, and if I don't like it, then I can re-frame it.

Frankly, given the difference in scale between us, I think it's best to petition the gods clearly, and give them a lot of wiggle room for their interpretation: they can see so much more than we can, that there's no point trying to cover everything anyway.

comments.title: Wonder
comments.content: How do I cultivate wonder in myself about the world around me?  I feel stagnant and closed off.

replies.title: Re: Wonder
replies.content:
Embrace boredom. I mean that quite literally. What keeps us stagnant and closed off is that our minds move too fast, flogged into motion by media and our own internal chatter. The only way to slow things down, and notice what's actually going on around us, is to do things that involve boredom.

I recall a story about a person who was convinced she couldn't write. Her teacher told her to look at a brick wall and write about it. She sat there, looking at the brick wall, and couldn't think of a word to write. Her teacher told her to move closer and write about a single brick. That was what it took for her to stop thinking and pay attention to the brick -- and her writer's block was broken.

That's why meditation is so valuable. It's literally the most boring activity in existence -- and that's why it's the door to mental clarity and wonder. Whether you choose that or some other path, slow down, and let yourself get bored enough to notice things.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sorry for being a few minutes late, no worries if this doesn't get put through:

I think that the story of writer's block cured by focusing on the brick facade was in Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance by Robert Pirsig.

replies.title: Re: pathworking
